content:
# Matthew Chapter 7 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 7 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 7:1 — "Judge not, that you be not judged" vs. "Do not judge, in order that you may not be judged" vs. "Don't judge others, and you won't be judged"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do not judge, in order that you may not be judged" | Literal with purpose clause made explicit |
| LOGOS | "Judge not, that you be not judged" | Traditional, formal phrasing |
| PNEUMA | "Don't judge others, and you won't be judged" | Dynamic — adds "others," changes purpose to consequence |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Judge not, that you be not judged" — this is one of the most recognized sayings of Jesus in English. The formal "Judge not" with "that" introducing the purpose clause is both accurate and deeply established. PNEUMA adds "others" (not in Greek) and recasts the purpose clause as a simple conditional.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Iconic phrasing preserved.

---

## 7:3 — "the speck" vs. "the splinter" for Greek κάρφος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the speck" | Standard rendering of κάρφος (a small dry particle) |
| LOGOS | "the speck" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the splinter" | "Splinter" — small piece of wood, creating a wood-to-wood parallel with δοκός |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the speck" — κάρφος refers to a small dry bit (straw, chaff, or similar). "Speck" is the established rendering and contrasts effectively with "log." PNEUMA's "splinter" creates a neat wood-wood parallel but narrows κάρφος to specifically wooden material.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Standard rendering maintained.

---

## 7:3-5 — "log" vs. "beam" vs. "plank" for Greek δοκός

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "beam" | Literal — δοκός is a construction beam or timber |
| LOGOS | "log" | Accessible English — large piece of wood |
| PNEUMA | "plank" | Another accessible rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "log" — while EMET's "beam" is closer to the architectural meaning of δοκός (a structural timber), "log" conveys the absurdity of the image more effectively in modern English. "Plank" (PNEUMA) is also acceptable but "log" is more established in this context.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Vivid absurdity captured.

---

## 7:6 — "pigs" vs. "swine" for Greek χοίρων

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the swine" | Traditional/formal rendering |
| LOGOS | "pigs" | Modern English |
| PNEUMA | "pigs" | Same modern English |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "pigs" — while "swine" has a longer tradition in biblical translation, "pigs" is the natural modern English word and is not a loss in register in this proverbial context.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Modern English preferred here.

---

## 7:7 — "Ask, and it will be given to you" vs. "Ask, and you will receive"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Ask, and it will be given to you" | Literal passive rendering |
| LOGOS | "Ask, and it will be given to you" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Ask, and you will receive" | Active voice — shifts from passive to active |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Ask, and it will be given to you" — the Greek uses the divine passive (δοθήσεται, "it will be given"), implying God as the giver without naming him. This is a significant theological construction. PNEUMA's active voice loses the divine passive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Divine passive preserved.

---

## 7:9 — "Or which one of you, if his son asks him for bread, will give him a stone?" vs. "Think about it: if your child asks for bread, would you hand him a stone?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Or what man is there of you, whom if the son of him asks [for] bread — a stone will he give to him?" | Literal with parenthetical syntax |
| LOGOS | "Or which one of you, if his son asks him for bread, will give him a stone?" | Formal, clear |
| PNEUMA | "Think about it: if your child asks for bread, would you hand him a stone?" | Adds "Think about it" — not in Greek; "child" for "son" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — clear, accurate, and rhetorical in the right way. PNEUMA adds "Think about it" which is not in the text, and uses "child" where the Greek has "son" (υἱός).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:11 — "who are evil" vs. "with all your flaws" for Greek πονηροὶ ὄντες

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "being evil" | Literal participial rendering |
| LOGOS | "who are evil" | Standard relative clause |
| PNEUMA | "with all your flaws" | Softened — "flaws" instead of "evil" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "who are evil" — the Greek πονηροί is strong: "evil, wicked." Jesus uses a deliberately stark contrast between human evil and divine goodness. PNEUMA's "with all your flaws" softens this to mere imperfection, losing the theological force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The stark contrast is preserved.

---

## 7:12 — "So whatever you wish that others would do to you, do also to them" vs. "So in everything, treat others the way you want to be treated"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "All things therefore, whatever you wish that the men should do to you, thus also you do to them" | Literal with fronted "all things" |
| LOGOS | "So whatever you wish that others would do to you, do also to them" | Formal, balanced |
| PNEUMA | "So in everything, treat others the way you want to be treated" | Modern, colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "So whatever you wish that others would do to you, do also to them" preserves the formal structure of the Golden Rule while being clear. PNEUMA's version is natural but loses the deliberate "wish... do" parallelism.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:13 — "the way is easy" vs. "the way is broad" vs. "the broad road" for Greek εὐρύχωρος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "broad [is] the way" | Literal — εὐρύχωρος means spacious, broad |
| LOGOS | "the way is easy" | Interprets breadth as ease of travel |
| PNEUMA | "the broad road" | Literal but modern syntax |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the way is easy" — while εὐρύχωρος literally means "spacious/broad," LOGOS's "easy" captures the figurative sense: the wide road is easy to travel because it requires no discipline. However, this is an interpretive choice. The contrast with τεθλιμμένη ("hard/compressed") in v.14 supports the ease/difficulty interpretation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS, noting the interpretive element.

---

## 7:14 — "the way is hard" vs. "the way is constricted" vs. "the difficult road" for Greek τεθλιμμένη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "constricted [is] the way" | Literal — τεθλιμμένη (from θλίβω, to press, compress) |
| LOGOS | "the way is hard" | Dynamic — compressed way = hard way |
| PNEUMA | "the difficult road" | Similar dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the way is hard" — τεθλιμμένη (perfect passive of θλίβω) literally means "compressed, narrow, constricted." In the context of a road, this means difficult to travel. "Hard" captures the experiential sense.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Experiential sense for road metaphor.

---

## 7:15 — "ravenous wolves" vs. "wolves ravenous" vs. "vicious wolves"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "wolves ravenous" | Postpositive adjective following Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "ravenous wolves" | Natural English adjective-noun order |
| PNEUMA | "vicious wolves" | "Vicious" — changes the image from hunger to cruelty |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "ravenous wolves" — ἅρπαγες means rapacious, ravenous, seizing prey. "Ravenous" is the precise rendering. PNEUMA's "vicious" shifts from hunger-driven predation to general cruelty.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Precise adjective.

---

## 7:16-17 — "fruits" vs. "what they produce" for Greek καρποί

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "fruits" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "fruits" | Standard rendering |
| PNEUMA | "what they produce" | Dynamic — dissolves the metaphor |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "fruits" — the fruit metaphor is central to Matthew's moral vocabulary (3:8, 7:16-20, 12:33, 21:43). Consistently using "fruit(s)" allows readers to track this important theme. PNEUMA's "what they produce" dissolves the specific metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Key Matthean metaphor preserved.

---

## 7:17-18 — "healthy tree / diseased tree" vs. "good tree / rotten tree"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "good tree / rotten tree" | ἀγαθόν = good; σαπρόν = rotten |
| LOGOS | "healthy tree / diseased tree" | Interprets the agricultural context |
| PNEUMA | "good tree / rotten tree" | Same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "healthy tree / diseased tree" — LOGOS's rendering captures the agricultural reality better. A σαπρόν tree is one that is internally corrupted, not merely "bad." "Diseased" captures this organic corruption. Similarly, "healthy" conveys the living vitality of a fruit-bearing tree.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Agricultural precision.

---

## 7:23 — "depart from me, you workers of lawlessness" vs. "Get away from me, you who practice lawlessness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "depart from me, those working the lawlessness" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "depart from me, you workers of lawlessness" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Get away from me, you who practice lawlessness" | "Get away" is colloquial; "practice" for ἐργαζόμενοι |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "depart from me, you workers of lawlessness" — "depart" (ἀποχωρεῖτε) is a formal command appropriate to eschatological judgment. PNEUMA's "Get away" is too colloquial for the gravity of the scene.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:24-27 — "the rock" vs. "solid rock" / "the floods" vs. "the rivers" / "beat on" vs. "hammered"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "upon the rock... the rivers came" | Literal — ποταμοί as rivers |
| LOGOS | "on the rock... the floods came" | "Floods" for ποταμοί in the context of destructive water |
| PNEUMA | "solid rock... the rivers rose... howled and hammered" | Added "solid"; "howled" and "hammered" intensify |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "floods" better captures the destructive role of ποταμοί in this parable (water that threatens a house is floodwater, not an ordinary river). PNEUMA's additions ("solid," "howled," "hammered") add dramatic intensity not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Contextually appropriate without additions.

---

## 7:27 — "great was the fall of it" vs. "What a spectacular ruin it was!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the fall of it was great" | Literal word order |
| LOGOS | "great was the fall of it" | Inverted for emphasis — formal English |
| PNEUMA | "What a spectacular ruin it was!" | Exclamatory paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "great was the fall of it" — the inverted word order creates emphasis appropriate to the climactic ending of the parable. PNEUMA's exclamatory rendering ("spectacular ruin") adds interpretive color not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Emphatic but restrained.

---

## 7:28 — "the crowds were astonished" vs. "the crowds were stunned"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the crowds were astonished" | Standard rendering of ἐξεπλήσσοντο |
| LOGOS | "the crowds were astonished" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the crowds were stunned" | "Stunned" — more visceral but modern |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the crowds were astonished" — ἐκπλήσσω means to be struck with astonishment. "Astonished" is the established rendering and carries appropriate weight. PNEUMA's "stunned" is adequate but slightly too physical/modern.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 7:29 — "as one who had authority, and not as their scribes" vs. "with an authority of his own — nothing like the scribes they were used to"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "as [one] having authority, and not as the scribes of them" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "as one who had authority, and not as their scribes" | Formal, standard |
| PNEUMA | "with an authority of his own — nothing like the scribes they were used to" | Expanded paraphrase adding "of his own" and "they were used to" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — clear, accurate, and concise. PNEUMA adds interpretive expansions ("of his own," "they were used to") not present in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Frequency | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion/intensification | 8 occurrences | Usually rejected; dramatic additions not in Greek |
| PNEUMA colloquializes formal/solemn language | 4 occurrences | Rejected in judgment/eschatological contexts |
| EMET awkward word order | 4 occurrences | Resolved in favor of natural English |
| LOGOS provides baseline | 13 occurrences | LOGOS most often adopted |
| Iconic phrases preserved | 2 occurrences | "Judge not" and Golden Rule in traditional form |

### Overall Pattern Analysis

Matthew 7 concludes the Sermon on the Mount with some of the most well-known passages in the Gospels: the speck and log, "Ask, seek, knock," the Golden Rule, the narrow gate, the tree and its fruit, "Lord, Lord," and the wise and foolish builders. SOPHIA's consensus notes stated that "all three translators are in strong agreement" — and indeed the disagreements here are primarily matters of register and vocabulary rather than substantive theological divergence.

The primary axis of disagreement remains PNEUMA's tendency to intensify, expand, and colloquialize. In a chapter filled with eschatological weight (workers of lawlessness, the great fall), LOGOS's formal register consistently proved more appropriate. PNEUMA's contributions were most valuable as exegetical commentary (the insight about Jesus's self-derived authority in v.29, the absurd comedy of the log-and-speck image) rather than as translation choices.

EMET's most notable contribution was preserving the divine passive in 7:7 ("it will be given") and the stark "being evil" (πονηροί) in 7:11, both of which were adopted.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
